algae_description: Fine green filamentous algae visible along the waterline and on plant stems near the surface — likely early thread algae or green hair algae colonizing during the nitrogen cycle. Consistent with a cycling tank developing its first biofilm layers.
substrate_condition: Light, pale substrate visible through the water column — appears relatively clean with no obvious debris accumulation visible in this angle.
concerns: Water column appears slightly hazy/milky — could be a bacterial bloom consistent with an active nitrogen cycle at Day 30, or fine substrate disturbance. Worth monitoring but expected at this stage.
Fine filamentous algae at the waterline — normal for a cycling tank with developing nutrients, but worth tracking if it spreads aggressively post-cycle.
narrative: Shot taken from below the waterline looking up toward the surface — a striking angle that catches two vivid red shrimp: one clinging to a vertical cable right at the waterline, and another visible in the right midground near what appears to be the filter housing. Equipment is clearly visible: a sponge filter on the right, a heater probe center-left, and cables running to the surface. LED light strips are reflected in the water surface above. The water column reads slightly hazy — textbook bacterial bloom territory for a tank on Day 30 of cycling.
owner_comment: 2 boys snacking on some duckweed. the blotches above the water are just on the glass, evidence of evaporation.

algae_description: Green algae or plant fragments visible throughout the water column — small green specks and filaments suspended mid-water and clinging to the glass near the waterline. A small clump of what appears to be java moss or similar fine-leafed aquatic plant is visible on the substrate mid-left. A tiny sprig of green plant matter is also visible on top of the mesh media bag.
substrate_condition: Dark granular substrate (appears to be aqua soil or lava rock pellets) covering the full tank floor. Appears relatively undisturbed with no heavy debris accumulation visible.
concerns: Water column shows notable cloudiness with suspended particulate matter — consistent with an active nitrogen cycle bacterial bloom, which is expected at Day 30 but worth monitoring for clearing
Significant amount of debris or plant fragment material suspended in the water column and clinging to the waterline glass — may indicate surface agitation is dispersing plant matter or biofilm
A large mesh bag (likely crushed coral, aragonite, or biological media) is sitting directly on the substrate in the midground — unclear if intentionally placed or if it's shifting
narrative: Day 30 of the nitrogen cycle and the tank is telling its story clearly: the water column is visibly hazy with suspended particles — the hallmark of a bacterial bloom in full swing, which is right on schedule. Two small shrimp are visible near the substrate on the left side, appearing active and unperturbed. Equipment is visible on the right — a sponge filter and what looks like a heater or thermometer with a blue handle — and a mesh media bag sits prominently on the substrate, possibly containing crushed coral or similar buffering media.

substrate_condition: Dark substrate, appears clean with minimal visible debris
narrative: One boldly colored red/orange-red Neocaridina shrimp is actively feeding or grazing at the surface among the floating plant roots — a healthy, confident behavior. The floating plants (likely frogbit or dwarf water lettuce) have grown into a dense, vibrant green canopy with long trailing roots that reach well into the water column, providing excellent cover and foraging surface for the colony.

substrate_condition: Clean dark granular substrate (likely lava rock or similar), with a few scattered light-colored debris pieces — possibly plant matter or biofilm flecks. No significant detritus accumulation visible.
algae_description: No visible algae growth on substrate or plants.
narrative: Three bright red cherry shrimp are visible on the open substrate — two in the midground and one closer to the plant cluster on the right — actively grazing or exploring the substrate surface. A lush clump of green aquatic moss (likely java moss or similar) anchors the right side of the frame, appearing healthy and full.
owner_comment: interesting feeding behavior, they were bumping each other off for the food

image_subject: equipment close-up — heater and sponge filter
substrate_condition: small dark granular substrate visible, appears clean and undisturbed
narrative: This is a close-up equipment shot showing a submersible heater mounted to the rear glass with a suction cup, alongside a multi-layer black foam sponge filter to its right — both submerged and apparently operational. The water is clear with no visible turbidity, and small bubbles are present along the heater's glass tube, suggesting it is actively heating. No plants or shrimp are visible in this tightly framed shot.
owner_comment: been noticing that all the algae which has accumulated on the water heater during cycling has been grazed
